skill.name: root-cause-tracing
skill.description: Trace deep software failures back through call chains, test setup, and invalid data flow to find the original trigger.
skill.summary: A debugging workflow for tracing symptoms back to the first invalid input or incorrect caller.
skill.author: DYAI2025

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command patterns, but most SKILL.md matches are Markdown examples, dot diagrams, or inline debugging prose. The confirmed risk is a runnable shell helper that discovers test files, executes npm test, and inspects user-selected filesystem paths; this is legitimate for debugging but should run only in trusted repositories.

medium_findings.title: Runnable Helper Executes Local Test Commands
medium_findings.description: The helper script builds a list of matching test files and runs npm test for each file. This is expected for a debugging bisection tool, but it executes repository test code and should be used only in trusted workspaces.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell script directly invokes npm test after discovering files with find. The behavior matches the skill purpose, but it is still real command execution in the user workspace.

low_findings.title: User-Supplied Filesystem Path Inspection
low_findings.description: The script checks whether a user-supplied pollution path exists and prints file metadata with ls. This can expose local path details in command output, but there is no evidence of network transfer or secret collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem checks are explicit and use user-provided input. The risk is limited because paths are quoted and results stay local.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Misclassified as Executable Code
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported many shell or backtick patterns in SKILL.md. These lines are documentation examples, dot diagrams, inline code spans, and debugging snippets rather than executable package code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious patterns are inside Markdown fenced blocks or inline examples. I found no evidence that SKILL.md itself executes those commands automatically.

low_findings.title: Documentation-Only Environment Reference
low_findings.description: SKILL.md references process.env.NODE_ENV inside a TypeScript logging example. This is not active code in the skill and does not read or transmit environment variables during skill installation or use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment reference is confined to a Markdown example. No executable JavaScript file or network sink was found in the analyzed files.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged description and tracing prose discuss debugging concepts, not cryptography, malware reconnaissance, or system inventory collection. No evidence found for weak cryptographic operations or hostile reconnaissance behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain skill metadata and ordinary debugging questions. I found no crypto API calls, credential discovery, host enumeration, or persistence behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Script Executes Project Test Runner
dangerous_patterns.description: find-polluter.sh invokes npm test for discovered files. This is a real external command pattern and should be disclosed to users before they run the helper.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is present in an executable shell script. It is intentional debugging behavior, not hidden execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Script Enumerates Matching Files
dangerous_patterns.description: find-polluter.sh uses find, sort, wc, and ls to enumerate test files and inspect pollution paths. These commands are expected but operate on the local filesystem.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file enumeration commands are directly visible and local. Arguments are quoted where they touch user input, reducing command injection concern.

content.user_title: Trace Bugs to Their Root Cause
content.value_statement: Deep failures often hide the first bad input or caller. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through backward tracing, instrumentation, and source-level fixes.
actual_capabilities: Guides backward tracing from a visible symptom to the original trigger.
Shows how to inspect call chains and invalid data flow.
Recommends targeted instrumentation before risky or failing operations.
Includes a helper script for locating tests that create unwanted files or state.
Encourages fixing the source cause and adding validation at boundaries.
limitations: The helper script is designed for Node.js projects that use npm test.
It does not automatically fix bugs or modify application code.
Manual judgment is still needed to interpret stack traces and call chains.
The shell helper should only run inside trusted repositories.

use_cases.title: Trace a Deep Runtime Failure
use_cases.description: Follow a failing operation backward through callers until the first incorrect value or decision is found.

use_cases.title: Find a Test That Pollutes State
use_cases.description: Run tests one by one to identify which test creates an unexpected file, directory, or state change.

use_cases.title: Review Debugging Strategy
use_cases.description: Use the workflow to decide where to add logging, validation, and defensive checks.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Root Cause Trace
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for a visible symptom.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use root-cause-tracing. The symptom is: [describe failure]. Help me trace backward from the failing operation to the first bad input.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze a Stack Trace
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate workflow for call chain analysis.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use root-cause-tracing on this stack trace: [paste trace]. Identify the caller chain, likely source trigger, and next evidence to collect.

prompt_templates.title: Add Focused Instrumentation
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate workflow when the caller is unclear.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use root-cause-tracing. Suggest minimal instrumentation for this operation: [operation]. Include what context to log and where to remove it later.

prompt_templates.title: Investigate Test Pollution
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow for unwanted test side effects.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use root-cause-tracing. A test run creates [file or state]. Plan a bisection approach and explain how to confirm the polluting test safely.

output_examples.input: A command initializes a repository in the wrong directory during a test run.
output_examples.output: The trace identifies the immediate operation, follows each caller, finds the empty directory value, and recommends validating the setup fixture before use.

output_examples.input: A full test suite leaves an unexpected directory behind, but single failures are unclear.
output_examples.output: The workflow proposes checking for the directory before each test, running candidate tests one at a time, and inspecting the first test that creates it.

output_examples.input: A stack trace shows a low-level filesystem error without a clear source.
output_examples.output: The output separates the symptom from the source, lists caller frames to inspect, and suggests logging the path, working directory, and triggering test name.

best_practices: Start from the exact symptom and trace one caller level at a time.
Log only the context needed to prove where a bad value began.
Fix the source cause, then add validation near important boundaries.
anti_patterns: Do not patch only the line where the error appears.
Do not leave temporary debug logging in production paths.
Do not run the helper script in untrusted repositories or unknown test suites.

faq.question: What problem does this skill solve?
faq.answer: It helps trace a visible failure back to the original bad input, caller, or test setup mistake.

faq.question: Does it change my code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides a debugging process and examples. You decide which code changes to make.

faq.question: Why does it include a shell script?
faq.answer: The script helps find which test creates unwanted files or state by running matching tests one at a time.

faq.question: Is the shell script safe to run?
faq.answer: Run it only in trusted projects. It executes npm test and inspects local paths that you provide.

faq.question: Can I use this outside Node.js projects?
faq.answer: Yes for the tracing method. The included helper script is specific to projects that use npm test.

faq.question: Does it require Claude Code?
faq.answer: No. The workflow can support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code when they can inspect the relevant project context.
